title: Path Traversal
description: Path traversal vulnerabilities allow attackers to navigate outside the intended directory using sequences like ../ in file path inputs, potentially accessing sensitive system files, configuration files, or private user data.
learning_objectives: Explain how ../ sequences enable directory traversal
Identify vulnerable file-handling patterns in web applications
Apply path resolution and base directory validation
Use allowlisting to restrict accessible files
Recognise path traversal risks in AI-generated file-serving code

sections.title: How Path Traversal Works
sections.content: When an application accepts a filename or path as user input and uses it to access the file system, an attacker can supply path sequences like ../ (go up one directory) to navigate outside the intended directory. The operating system resolves these sequences as part of normal path handling — the application has no way to detect the traversal unless it explicitly validates the resolved path.
sections.key_concept: The ../ sequence is not a special attack string — it is valid file system syntax that the OS resolves normally. The vulnerability is allowing user input to control where on the file system the application reads.

sections.title: Real-World Attack Scenario
sections.scenario: A document management system allows authenticated users to download files by passing a filename in the URL: /download?file=report.pdf. The application joins this value with an upload directory path. An attacker crafts the request: /download?file=../../../../etc/passwd. The OS resolves this to the system password file path, and the application serves its contents — exposing every system user account.
impact: Read sensitive system files: /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, SSH private keys
Read application configuration files containing database credentials or API keys
Read other users' private files if the application stores them on the same server
In write-vulnerable scenarios: overwrite configuration files or place malicious scripts

vulnerable.code:
# VULNERABLE: joins user input directly to base path
import os
from flask import send_file, request, abort

UPLOAD_DIR = '/var/app/uploads'

@app.route('/download')
def download():
    filename = request.args.get('file')
    # os.path.join does NOT prevent traversal
    filepath = os.path.join(UPLOAD_DIR, filename)
    return send_file(filepath)

# ?file=../../../../etc/passwd
# filepath = '/var/app/uploads/../../../../etc/passwd'
# Resolves to: '/etc/passwd'
vulnerable.problem: os.path.join does not resolve or validate traversal sequences. The resulting path can point anywhere on the file system.

secure.fix: os.path.realpath() resolves all ../ sequences and symlinks to an absolute canonical path. The startswith check confirms the resolved path is still inside the intended directory — any traversal attempt lands outside and is rejected.

vulnerable.problem: path.join normalises the path but does not prevent traversal above the base directory when the input contains ../ sequences.

secure.fix: path.resolve() produces an absolute canonical path. The startsWith check ensures the file being served is inside the uploads directory, regardless of how many ../ sequences were in the input.

sections.title: Security-by-Design Principles
sections.content: The safest approach is to never allow user input to directly control a file system path. Where file access is required, use indirect references.

principles.title: Use Indirect File References
principles.detail: Instead of accepting a filename from the user, accept an ID that maps to a filename in your database. The user never touches the actual file path.

principles.title: Allowlist Permitted Files or Extensions
principles.detail: If the filename must come from user input, validate it against an allowlist of permitted names or file extensions before constructing the path.

principles.title: Apply Least Privilege to File System Access
principles.detail: Run the web application process with a user account that only has read access to the directories it needs. Even if path traversal occurs, the process cannot read files outside its permitted scope.
principles.example:
# Run Flask as a restricted user (in deployment)
# The app user should only have read access to /var/app/uploads
# Not to /etc, /home, or other sensitive directories

items.text: Never use user-supplied input directly in file system path construction
Use os.path.realpath() or path.resolve() to canonicalise paths before use
Always validate that the resolved path begins with the intended base directory
Prefer indirect references (database IDs) over direct filenames where possible
Allowlist permitted file extensions and reject paths containing separators
Run the application with the minimum file system permissions required

sections.title: Watch Out in AI-Generated Code
sections.content: AI assistants frequently generate file download or serving endpoints using os.path.join() or path.join() with user input, without adding path validation. This is one of the most consistently generated path traversal vulnerabilities.
red_flags: os.path.join(BASE_DIR, user_input) with no subsequent validation
send_file(request.args.get('file')) — user input passed directly to file serving
open(filename, 'r') where filename comes from request parameters
No check that the resolved path starts with the intended base directory
Using str.replace('../', '') as sanitisation — can be bypassed with ....// patterns

quiz.question: An application builds a file path as: os.path.join('/uploads', request.args.get('file')). What input would allow an attacker to read /etc/passwd?
options: Both B and C depending on URL encoding handling
quiz.explanation: Both ../../../etc/passwd and its URL-encoded equivalent ..%2F..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd may work depending on whether the application decodes the input before or after path construction. Robust validation must be applied after full path resolution.

quiz.question: Which function correctly defends against path traversal by canonicalising a file path?
quiz.explanation: os.path.realpath() resolves all ../ sequences and symlinks to produce the absolute canonical path. os.path.basename() only extracts the filename component — it doesn't prevent traversal if the input contains path separators within the name. String replacement can be bypassed with patterns like ....//.

quiz.question: What is the most robust architectural approach to preventing path traversal in a file download feature?
options: Validate that the filename contains no special characters
Use indirect file references — accept a database ID and look up the path server-side
Convert all filenames to lowercase before use
Restrict file access to files smaller than 10MB
quiz.explanation: Using indirect references means the user never provides a file path — they provide an ID, and the server looks up the trusted path in a database. This eliminates the attack surface entirely, as user input never influences which file system path is accessed.
